data.title: Singapore minister apologises to Hong Kong government after football ‘idiots’ insult
data.description:
David Neo, the acting Singapore sports minister, has privately apologised to the Hong Kong government after he was captured on camera labelling the city’s football players and fans “idiots” following this week’s Asian Cup qualifier at Kai Tak Stadium.
Singapore won 2-1 to reach the 2027 finals at the expense of Hong Kong.
An Instagram live stream from one of the visiting players’ accounts showed Neo, the city state’s acting minister for culture, community and youth, telling a jubilant away dressing room: “It was fantastic … you kena [Malay for hit] pressured by them, all the fans were bloody idiots, end up players also played like idiots [sic] … But you all played like lions.”
Appointed to parliament in May, Neo quickly responded to an Instagram user who had called his comments “out of order”, although he stopped short of apologising.
“Indeed I probably should have been more respectful,” he said. “I take back what I said. The Hong Kong team were really tough and their fans were fully behind them. That’s something we should respect, and let’s also get behind our Lions.”

Nonetheless, Neo has privately said sorry to Rosanna Law Shuk-pui, the Hong Kong sports minister, according to a spokeswoman for the government’s Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau.
“Singapore’s Acting Minister for Culture, Community and Youth has not only publicly apologised and retracted his remarks, but has also expressed his apologies via email to the Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism of the Hong Kong SAR Government,” said the spokeswoman.
“The Secretary [Law] stated that the incident will not affect the long-standing relationship between Hong Kong and Singapore in the fields of culture and sports.”
Singapore stunned a highly charged Kai Tak crowd of 47,762 after they mustered two second-half goals to cancel out Matt Orr’s 15th-minute opener.
Their players and around 1,000 travelling supporters celebrated wildly at full time, after Singapore had secured Asian Cup qualification for the first time. Their only previous finals appearance was as hosts in 1984.
The result has had significant ramifications for Hong Kong. A number of players are the wrong side of 30 and unlikely to have another shot at going to a major tournament, while the Post has been told that Ashley Westwood’s 15-month reign as head coach is poised to come to an end.

data.title: Hong Kong’s new openings, from the East Kowloon Cultural Centre to a new pasta bar
data.description:
Twist Pasta Bar

From the minds behind Honky Tonks Tavern and Mendel’s, the city’s latest pasta bar can be found in the form of Twist, with its made-for-Instagram interiors and hearty dishes such as bone marrow macaroni, a twist on Kau Kee’s famous curry brisket, and squid ink spaghetti with dried shrimp roe from Yau Kee. Beyond pasta, desserts such as the white chocolate and soy milk panna cotta pay homage to the local pudding tofu fa.
17 Bridges Street, Central
MoA Dining

Formerly known as Hue, the Woolly Pig group’s venue at the Hong Kong Museum of Art reopens as MoA Dining, pairing harbour views with modern Mediterranean fare. The Tsim Sha Tsui hotspot nods to local flavours with dishes such as a Hong Kong-style bouillabaisse alongside suckling pig and paella. The bright, art-filled room (look for works by Tracey Emin, Damien Hirst and Rocco Ritchie) also features an artisanal bread trolley.
1/F, Hong Kong Museum of Art, 10 Salisbury Road, Tsim Sha Tsui
La Baia

Bringing Mediterranean coastal flair to Kai Tak Sports Park, La Baia is designed to transport diners to the likes of Palma and Mykonos with its woven fabric installations, earthy tones and handwoven blue and brown seating. Offering rotating seasonal dishes, Baia’s menu showcases freshly made pizzas, pastas and classic antipasti such as calamari fritti and sea urchin aglio olio.
G/F, Dining Cove, Kai Tak Sports Park, Kai Tak
East Kowloon Cultural Centre

Designed by architect Rocco Yim Sen-kee, the East Kowloon Cultural Centre ushers in a new chapter for Hong Kong’s arts scene with five cutting-edge venues, including The Hall, with 1,200 seats, and The Theatre, with 536 seats, both of which are designed for large-scale and immersive productions. The debut season features 17 programmes, including the original Cantonese musical HighLights: The Memories of Charles Kao and internationally acclaimed acts such as Lucie in The Sky and Alt-Escape.
60 Ngau Tau Kok Road, Kowloon

data.title: Could Osaka be Japan’s second capital? Takaichi appears to demur on ally’s plan
data.description:
When Japan’s then incoming leader Sanae Takaichi was scrambling in October for a new coalition partner so the Liberal Democratic Party could remain in power, she seemed open to requests put forth by the Japan Innovation Party in return for its support.
One of the key demands of the JIP was that Osaka should be officially elevated in status to the nation’s “second capital”, with additional infrastructure for the city to enable it to become the seat of government in the event of a natural disaster crippling Tokyo.
Hirofumi Yoshimura, governor of Osaka prefecture since 2019 and head of the JIP for nearly a year, emerged from a meeting with senior LDP leaders in October and declared that Takaichi had said she would be “willing to work” with him on the “second capital” plan.
While the two parties have begun discussions on the idea and there are vague suggestions on how to turn Osaka into Japan’s second capital, the JIP is doing all the running. And questions about the feasibility – and desirability – of the plan are quietly being asked.
With Takaichi cemented now as prime minister, analysts say she appears to have gone cool on the idea.
And that has left the JIP in a delicate position on whether it should stick with the alliance and hope the LDP would be aligned with the plan in future, or walk away from the ruling party if it is getting cold feet.

Despite the JIP’s dominant position in the city, it has lost two referendums – in 2015 and again in 2020 – after failing to get support from voters for its “Osaka Metropolis Plan”, as part of its ambition to position the city as the nation’s second capital.
If implemented, this plan would have led to the dissolution of Osaka City as a single municipal entity and reorganisation of its 24 administrative wards into a few large, semi-autonomous special wards, similar to the system in Tokyo.
“This debate has been going on for some time in Osaka already, but the JIP has already failed with votes previously,” said Hiromi Murakami, a professor of political science at the Tokyo campus of Temple University.
“The results were very close each time, but the people of Osaka rejected a proposal that is in many ways the party’s raison d’etre,” she told This Week in Asia. The JIP’s hope, she added, was that an alliance with the LDP might give the proposal the impetus required to get it over the line.
The first referendum fell short by less than 1 per cent of the votes cast, with the plan defeated in the second referendum by a similarly narrow 1.2 per cent margin.
Voters were not convinced by the JIP’s claims that the change would streamline the bloated bureaucracy by eliminating overlapping layers at the city and local level, reducing costs and enhancing efficiency.
Opponents of the capital plan – which included Osaka’s LDP chapter acting in concert with the Japanese Communist Party – successfully argued that the proposed changes would cause revenue shortfalls, worsen local services, especially in less well-off parts of the city, and centralise power and decision-making at the prefecture level to the detriment of local administrations.

“The JIP really wanted this from the LDP because if they were able to go back to their supporters and say they had achieved their goal, that they had done what they set out to do and that the party could then build on this, then that would have been a success,” Murakami said.
“And Takaichi and the LDP were obviously not as keen on the plan, but they agreed to it in return for the coalition. But with the LDP backing away, the only conclusion is that this is a disaster for the JIP. They have lost a lot of face.”
Murakami pointed out that the LDP had “never been the party of reform”, and it traditionally would not back measures calling for smaller bureaucracies.
Stephen Nagy, a professor of international relations at Tokyo’s International Christian University, said the LDP had other motivations to go back on its word.
“The JIP is very much a regional party and it has a vision of a more decentralised Japan that will contribute to the economic growth of Osaka,” he said. “There is also the symbolism of moving some of the power out of Tokyo. None of that appeals to the LDP.”
Osaka is traditionally a more liberal city, and the LDP would resist any political manoeuvres that might weaken its control or grant additional powers to another party, even one that is an ally, according to Nagy.
“There is a clear political component here and the LDP has no intention of diluting its own powers.”

Conservative-leaning media outlets have poured cold water on the JIP’s capital plan, with an editorial on November 19 in the Yomiuri newspaper dismissing it as “difficult to understand”.
In response to the JIP’s suggestions put forward in its discussions with the LDP, the Yomiuri said its proposal “appears designed to favour Osaka prefecture, the party’s home turf”. It also criticised the JIP’s call for infrastructure development, suggesting it “brought a sense of discomfort”.
Yomiuri also dismissed the argument for Osaka to be a backup for Tokyo in the event of a natural disaster, saying that the city faced the possibility of a major earthquake in the offshore Nankai Trough of between 60 per cent and 90 per cent in the next 30 years.
“To disperse the risks from disasters, it does not seem necessary to limit relocation sites for ministries and agencies to a major city. This is likely an issue to be considered separately from the matter of the second capital.”

data.title: Frida Kahlo’s self-portrait, sold for US$54.7m, breaks auction record for female artists
data.description:
A haunting 1940 self-portrait by famed Mexican artist Frida Kahlo sold Thursday for US$54.7 million and became the top-selling work by any female artist at an auction.
The painting of Kahlo asleep in a bed — titled “El sueno (La cama)” or in English, “The Dream (The Bed)” — surpassed the record held by Georgia O’Keeffe’s “Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1,” which sold for US$44.4 million in 2014.
The sale at Sotheby’s in New York also topped Kahlo’s own auction record for a work by a Latin American artist. The 1949 painting “Diego and I,” depicting the artist and her husband, muralist Diego Rivera, went for US$34.9 million in 2021. Her paintings are reported to have sold privately for even more.
The self-portrait is among the few Kahlo pieces that have remained in private hands outside Mexico, where her body of work has been declared an artistic monument. Her works in both public and private collections within the country cannot be sold abroad or destroyed.
The painting comes from a private collection, whose owner has not been disclosed, and is legally eligible for international sale. Some art historians have scrutinized the sale for cultural reasons, while others have raised concern that the painting — last exhibited publicly in the late 1990s — could again disappear from public view after the auction. It has already been requested for upcoming exhibitions in cities including New York, London and Brussels.
The buyer’s identity was not disclosed.
The piece depicts Kahlo asleep in a wooden, colonial-style bed that floats in the clouds. She is draped in a golden blanket and entangled in crawling vines and leaves. Above the bed lies a skeleton figure wrapped in dynamite.

Kahlo vibrantly and unsparingly depicted herself and events from her life, which was upended by a bus accident at 18. She started to paint while bedridden, underwent a series of painful surgeries on her damaged spine and pelvis, then wore casts until her death in 1954 at age 47.
During the years Kahlo was confined to her bed, she came to view it as a bridge between worlds as she explored her mortality.
Before the auction, her great-niece, Mara Romeo Kahlo, celebrated the significance of the upcoming sale during a recent interview with The Associated Press in Mexico City.
“I’m very proud that she’s one of the most valued women, because really, what woman doesn’t identify with Frida, or what person doesn’t?” she said. “I think everyone carries a little piece of my aunt in their heart.”
The painting was the star of a sale of more than 100 surrealist works by artists including Salvador Dalí, René Magritte, Max Ernst and Dorothea Tanning.
Kahlo resisted being labeled a surrealist, a style of art that’s dreamlike and centers on a fascination with the unconscious mind.
“I never painted dreams,” she once said. “I painted my own reality.”
In its catalog note, Sotheby’s said the painting “offers a spectral meditation on the porous boundary between sleep and death.”
“The suspended skeleton is often interpreted as a visualization of her anxiety about dying in her sleep, a fear all too plausible for an artist whose daily existence was shaped by chronic pain and past trauma,” the catalog notes.

Earlier this week, a Gustav Klimt painting that helped save the life of its Jewish subject during the Holocaust sold at Sotheby’s for US$236.4 million. Klimt’s “Portrait of Elisabeth Lederer” became one of the most expensive pieces of artwork ever sold at auction, second only to Leonardo da Vinci’s “Salvator Mundi” at US$450 million — the record-holder over all and among male artists.

data.title: K-drama casting news about Shin Hye-sun, Na In-woo, Ji Chang-wook and more
data.description:
A cross-cultural romance, a fantastical connection, a gripping historical drama and a crime-comedy-mystery are among the latest projects attracting Korea’s top acting talent.
Here’s the K-drama casting round-up for this week.
1. 1/24
Shin Hye-sun and Na In-woo have been approached to appear in a new romantic comedy together. They are mulling offers to appear in 1/24, a Netflix original series based on the webtoon An Hour of Romance.
The story follows a male tennis ace and a female reality show producer, both harbouring emotional scars, who experience a fantastical event that sees them swap bodies for one hour each day. While navigating this phenomenon, the pair begin to learn about one another, eventually falling in love.

Known for the hit period drama Mr. Queen, Shin recently starred in Welcome to Samdal-ri and Dear Hyeri. She has two coming shows: the Netflix drama The Art of Sarah alongside Lee Joon-hyuk, and the tvN series Secret Audit with Gong Myung.
Shin and Na have worked together before, as Na also appeared in Mr. Queen when he was an emerging actor. He has since become a leading star, known for shows like Jinxed at First and Marry My Husband.
Directing the show will be Kim Sang-hyub, known for the previous hit webtoon adaptation True Beauty and who most recently worked on Melancholia.
2. Merry Berry Love
Currently on screens in The Manipulated, Ji Chang-wook is set to appear in yet another Disney+ production, although this new project will mark a departure from the thrillers he has been making with the House of Mouse.

Merry Berry Love is a bright romantic comedy that will be co-produced by Korea’s CJ ENM and Japan’s Nippon TV, and will see Ji team up with rising Japanese actress Mio Imada.
Set on a remote island, the series will follow Korean spatial designer Lee Yoo-bin (played by Ji) and Japanese strawberry farmer Karin Shirahama (Imada).
In addition to Merry Berry Love, Ji is also set to join forces with Jun Ji-hyun for the high-profile fantasy romantic comedy Human X Gumiho. First, Ji will appear in the Netflix drama Scandals alongside Son Ye-jin and Nana.

Imada is known for the Tokyo Revengers films and the recent drama Trillion Game in Japan.
Kim Soo-jung, known for the hit BL (boy love) drama Semantic Error, will direct, while The Divorce Insurance scribe Lee Jae-yoon will pen the script.
3. Munmu
Lee Hyun-wook (Mine), Jang Hyuk (Family: The Unbreakable Bond) and Kim Kang-woo (The Tyrant) are set to headline the period drama Munmu, which will follow the formation of the Unified Silla Dynasty (668-935).

Lee will play Kim Pom-min, the Silla Prince and strategist who would become known as Munmu, the unifier of the three kingdoms of Silla, Goguryeo and Baekje. Jang will play Yeon Gaesomun, the dictatorial leader of Goguryeo, while Kim will appear as Pom-min’s father, the Silla king Kim Chun-chu.
Also tapped to appear are Park Sung-woong (Bloodhounds) as Silla general Kim Yu-sin; Jung Woong-in (Trigger) as a veiled political power within the Silla court; and Cho Sung-ha (Queen Mantis) as Goguryeo war hero Go Geon-mu, who returns from war to peacefully rule Goguryeo before a bloody coup.
The series will be brought to life by Kim Yeong-jo, who previously served as a co-director on the period epic Hwarang: The Poet Warrior Youth.
4. Fifties Professionals
Shin Ha-kyun (Beyond Evil), Oh Jung-se (It’s Okay to Not Be Okay) and Heo Sung-tae (Squid Game season 1) are confirmed as the leads of the coming MBC drama Fifties Professionals.

The series follows a trio of middle-aged men who, long after their glory days, were exiled to remote Yeongseon Island due to a mysterious incident. But then events conspire to bring them back into action as they band together to uncover the mystery that occurred 10 years ago.
Shin will play Jung Ho-myung, a former black agent at the National Intelligence Service who was framed for a crime he did not commit and now works as a chef in a Chinese restaurant.
Oh is slated to play North Korean special agent Bong Je-soon, who has lost his memory. Heo will feature as former gangland kingpin Kang Beom-ryong, who now runs a convenience store.
Fifties Professionals will be directed by Han Dong-hwa (Shadow Detective) and written by Jang Won-seob. The series is expected to launch in the first half of 2026.

data.title: Hong Kong director Andrew Lau reveals inspiration for National Games opening ceremony
data.description:
Acclaimed Hong Kong filmmaker Andrew Lau Wai-keung, chief director of the National Games opening ceremony in Guangzhou on November 9, has revealed the event was designed not just for mainland viewers, but as a heartfelt showcase of Chinese culture and unity for the global Chinese diaspora, many of whom were grappling with their own post-1997 stories.
Lau is famous for directing classic Hong Kong films such as the Infernal Affairs trilogy, Legend of the Fist, the Young and Dangerous series and many more.
Speaking to the Post in an exclusive interview, Lau detailed his role as chief director of the 15th National Games opening ceremony, explaining how he infused the deep spirit of Lingnan, or Southern China, and Cantonese culture into the spectacle.
“The major theme of the event was ‘One Heart, One Wish’ … it was very simple but very good – we wholeheartedly wanted to unite our Greater Bay Area,” he said.
“It was a good opportunity for all the people of the so-called Greater Bay Area to band together to do something.”
He added: “We are also putting on a display for the whole world because there are many Chinese people all over the world.
“The event was broadcast to many places and possibly over a billion people watched it, making it possible to spread our Chinese culture outwards.”

Discussing one of the ceremony’s most talked-about elements – the giant installation of the mythical “Ao” fish featuring a dragon’s head and the body of a winged fish – Lau credited Art Director Shang Tianbao with the idea, calling the figure highly representative.
“The Ao fish really represents inspiration,” he explained, noting its design was based on the traditional principle of the “Carp Leaping Over the Dragon Gate”, a profound aspect of Cantonese culture.
Lau described the creation of the massive fish as a gruelling process, involving traditional weaving techniques rather than simple painting.
To achieve the effect of it “rising into the air”, the team used significant amounts of nitrogen gas.

Furthermore, he highlighted the contribution of the twenty young martial arts students who “trained day and night”.
He said their dedication transformed the apparatus from “a rather lifeless thing” into something “very vivid, very lovable, and lifelike”.
As the representative of the South, Lau emphasised his push to integrate specific Cantonese cultural elements.
He recalled the first planning meeting with directors and consultants from across the country, where he demonstrated the local martial arts.
“After you show it to them, they grasp the feeling,” he said, citing Wong Fei-hung, Yingge dance, the southern Chinese martial art style of Hung Kuen, and Wing Chun as essential regional elements.
Other features, such as Foshan ceramics and Cantonese food, were also key components, alongside Guangzhou’s historical role as the starting point of the Maritime Silk Road.
The prevalence of water in the performance, Lau explained, is directly linked to the geography of the Greater Bay Area.
“Hong Kong is surrounded by sea on four sides, Macau too, and the Pearl River … Everyone agreed water was very suitable,” he said, but acknowledged its addition presented significant challenges.
During rehearsals, the large water surface made the ground slippery, a difficult issue to manage, requiring anti-slip shoes and constant adjustment.
Yet, he said that during the actual opening night, “not a single person slipped... I think that was quite miraculous”.

Lau deliberately ensured that Cantonese was prominently featured and that his own speeches and interviews during the preparation were conducted in Cantonese, given the event’s location.
He also expressed gratitude to the local artists, including Joey Yung Cho-yee, Andy Lau Tak-wah, Jordan Chan Siu-chun and Jerry Lamb Hiu-fung, who performed mostly in Cantonese, in particular, the song “Boundless Oceans Vast Skies”.
“Some people even refer to that song as our ‘provincial anthem’,” Lau joked, referring to the song’s immense popularity in the Cantonese community.
“But actually, the entire country knows that song and can sing along.”
He felt their collective effort fostered an “ardent atmosphere”, underscoring the simple realisation: “We are all Chinese people, I think it’s that simple.”
When asked about the changes in Hong Kong since the handover, Lau shared personal reflections, speaking of families who emigrated ahead of the handover, and that his own decision to stay and develop his career in Hong Kong was the correct one for him.
“You see how I’m doing now, and it’s a good example,” Lau said, asserting that Hong Kong people have achieved great things since 1997.
He stressed that the ceremony was a display for the global Chinese community, regardless of their location, showcasing their shared heritage.
Finally, Lau touched upon the opening of the upcoming Para Games, which will focus on how able-bodied people and disabled people can “mutually help each other”, sending a message of “mutual care and mutual assistance” that he found deeply moving.
“That event will have an entirely different vibe, which is love and warmth,” he said.

data.title: Taiwan military budgets US$32 million for drone tech amid Beijing’s pressure
data.description:
Taiwan’s military plans to allocate more than NT$1 billion (US$32 million) for drone technology integration as the island scales up its uncrewed platforms and bolsters asymmetric warfare capabilities amid growing pressure from Beijing.
According to a budget document recently submitted to Taiwan’s legislature, the Armaments Bureau will spend more than NT$1.01 billion from 2026 to 2028 on a “forward-looking multi-integration development project” for uncrewed platforms, with NT$507 million to be executed in the first year.
The initiative aims to develop core technologies that would allow drones to operate without Global Positioning System (GPS) support, resist jamming and use high-density batteries – capabilities seen as critical in a conflict with Beijing.
It would also integrate interministerial resources and academic-industrial research capacity to promote indigenous development of uncrewed platforms, defence systems and surface and underwater vehicles.
The goal is to build an innovative research and development and manufacturing cluster, as well as strengthen Taiwan’s emerging drone industry ecosystem.

The programme covers technologies ranging from electro-optical lasers and composite materials to anti-jamming communications, navigation and positioning, uncrewed platforms and low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite systems.
Next year’s priorities include detailed design and testing for underwater optical-reconnaissance systems, a numerical-simulation platform for LEO-based positioning and the establishment of production capability for medium- and large-scale continuous-fibre composites, according to the programme.
“The Russia–Ukraine war has served as a warning that uncrewed platforms can have a highly asymmetric impact on modern battlefields,” Wellington Koo Li-hsiung, Taiwan’s defence minister, told lawmakers on November 6 as he briefed them on the military’s drone deployment plans.
Koo said the armed forces had shifted their development focus to low-cost, expendable drones that could be mass produced and were capable of immediate reconnaissance-strike missions.
“In peacetime, drones will share high-risk tasks such as surveillance and patrols,” he said. “In wartime, they will work with major weapons systems to conduct precision strikes to deny, delay and disrupt any attempt by an enemy to attack Taiwan.”
Koo added that the defence ministry would refine training standards, set up dedicated units, improve acquisition channels and incorporate drones into joint exercises from the second half of next year.
The Armaments Bureau’s project forms part of a broader government effort to build a vertically integrated drone sector supplying both military and civilian agencies. The objective is to eliminate reliance on a mainland China or “red” supply chain and drive more diverse applications at home.
In August, Taiwan’s defence ministry requested NT$1.19 billion to procure multipurpose administrative-use drones for the army, navy, air force, military police and political warfare bureau.
Beyond defence spending, Taiwan’s cabinet in October approved a six-year NT$44.2 billion plan to build an uncrewed systems industrial ecosystem covering drones, unstaffed boats, and surface and subsurface vehicles.
The plan includes the purchase of 50,898 drones for civil and government use and 48,750 for defence purposes over the next three years.
At a recent virtual panel hosted by the Centre for a New American Security, a Washington-based think tank, analysts said Taiwan was moving rapidly to expand its arsenal of military and commercial drones.


According to Hong-Lun Tiunn, a non-resident fellow at the Research Institute for Democracy, Society and Emerging Technology in Taipei, Beijing was dominating the supply for “a lot of different components that are used in drones”.
Tiunn believes it is therefore crucial for Taiwan to build a “non-reliant supply chain” to ensure it can produce sufficient high-quality components and platforms to integrate into its layered defence strategy in the event of a cross-strait conflict.
Beijing has significantly increased pressure on Taiwan, including large-scale military drills around the island, since 2016, when Tsai Ing-wen of the independence-leaning Democratic Progressive Party came to power. She served two terms as Taiwan’s leader.
Tsai’s successor, William Lai Ching-te, led the DPP to victory in the 2024 election. Beijing has described Lai as a “diehard Taiwan separatist” and “troublemaker”.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Meanwhile, Taiwan has sought to cultivate a “non-red” supply chain with American and European partners while aiming to lift output at home to at least NT$40 billion by 2030.
Taiwanese suppliers have been racing to position themselves for an unprecedented wave of orders. Their ranks include Aerospace Industrial Development Corporation and Geosat Aerospace & Technology as well as electronics manufacturers expanding into motors, sensors and communications.
But some industry figures have warned that Taiwan’s commercial base remains fragmented and too focused on hardware, with local production of key components such as specialised drone chips lacking.
“Taiwan’s understanding of the drone industry is still stuck in a hardware-centric mindset,” said Geosat chairman Lo Cheng-fang, who also heads the Asia UAV AI Innovation Application R&D Centre in Chiayi, in southwestern Taiwan.
“If Taiwan deploys 100,000 drones within the next two years, how will they be managed? Where will the imagery be stored? How will data be exchanged?” he asked.
Lo said larger drones being developed internationally – such as Turkey’s next-generation Bayraktar TB3, an uncrewed combat vehicle built for longer endurance, greater payload capacity and higher speeds – would require AI-enabled avionics and far more complex flight-control systems. He questioned whether Taiwan was “prepared for that”.


Analysts said the biggest risks in Taiwan’s drone tech push were not only in manufacturing capacity but in how the armed forces planned to absorb the volume of uncrewed systems.
Chieh Chung, a research fellow at the Association of Strategic Foresight in Taipei, said the military had yet to release a full doctrine, operating manuals or spectrum-management plans for drone deployment, despite preparing to field tens of thousands of platforms.
“The military must first know how it intends to fight,” Chieh said. “Only then can it draft equipment specifications and training pipelines. Otherwise, communications, jamming resistance and even basic coordination could fall apart in a real conflict.”
Su Tzu-yun, a senior analyst at the military-affiliated Institute for National Defence and Security Research in Taipei, argued that Taiwan should consider establishing a semi-official drone-chip design firm to reduce dependence on foreign suppliers and secure opportunities in the global non-red supply chain.
Industry groups said the island’s drone firms – mostly small and medium-sized manufacturers – continued to struggle with financing long certification processes and worked in a procurement system that historically favoured foreign equipment.
They also cautioned that Taiwan had yet to develop unified standards for large-scale deployment, including data management infrastructure, inter-agency compatibility and certification schemes aligned with the US, European Union and Japan.
As Taiwan accelerates drone development to deter Beijing from possible military action, the Foundation for Defence of Democracies (FDD), a Washington-based think tank, has highlighted a parallel vulnerability: an “energy siege”.
In a report released on November 17, the FDD warned that Beijing could seek to paralyse the island long before drones or missiles come into play, namely by disrupting shipments of liquefied natural gas (LNG), which fuels about half of Taiwan’s electricity generation.
The FDD, which ran a tabletop exercise in Taiwan this summer, said Beijing was likely to employ a “grey-zone” strategy using port inspection delays, cyberattacks or pressure on foreign suppliers rather than impose an overt blockade.
The think tank recommended that the US prepare to “reflag” commercial tankers bound for Taiwan and have naval vessels escort them to raise the costs of any coercive action by Beijing.
Under international law, the report noted, the US had the right to protect vessels flying its flag on the high seas. Reflagging tankers would extend that security umbrella.
It also urged Taiwan to retain a nuclear option, including next-generation small modular reactors, to withstand possible disruptions to energy imports.

data.title: US aviation agency warns airlines flying over Venezuela of potential hazards
data.description:
The US Federal Aviation Administration warned major airlines on Friday of a “potentially hazardous situation” when flying over Venezuela and urged them to exercise caution.
The FAA notice cited the “worsening security situation and heightened military activity in or around Venezuela” and said threats could pose risks for aircraft at all altitudes.
There has been a massive American military build-up in the region in recent months, including the US Navy’s largest aircraft carrier, at least eight other warships and F-35 aircraft.
The administration of US President Donald Trump has bombed boats allegedly carrying drugs that have departed from the shoreline of Venezuela and other Latin American countries.
Direct flights by US passenger or cargo carriers to Venezuela have been suspended since 2019, but some US airlines fly over the country for some South American flights.
American Airlines said on Friday it had stopped overflying Venezuela in October. Delta Air Lines said it had stopped flying over Venezuela “a while ago”. United Airlines did not immediately comment.

The order requires US airlines to provide the FAA with at least 72 hours' advance notice of planned flights, but stopped short of prohibiting flights over the country.
The FAA said since September, there had been an increase in Global Navigation Satellite System interference in Venezuela, which in some cases caused lingering effects throughout a flight, as well as “activity associated with increasing Venezuela military readiness”.
“Venezuela has conducted multiple military exercises and directed the mass mobilisation of thousands of military and reserve forces,” the FAA said, adding that at no point had Venezuela expressed an intent to target civil aviation.
The agency said the Venezuelan military possessed advanced fighter aircraft and multiple weapons systems capable of reaching or exceeding civil aircraft operating altitudes, and there was potential low-altitude risk from air defence systems and anti-aircraft artillery.
The FAA said it would continue to monitor the risk environment for US civil aviation operating in the region.

data.title: Hong Kong’s AI education quest needs human connection to succeed
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
In a recent Chinese-language blog post, Hong Kong’s undersecretary for education Dr Jeff Sze Chun-fai wrote about the city’s digital education blueprint. We at Just Feel welcome the government’s commitment to strengthening the city’s artificial intelligence-related competencies, enhancing teachers’ professional development and expanding resources for schools. But as Hong Kong advances its digital transformation, one principle deserves equal weight: meaningful learning, whether for teachers or students, is fundamentally relational.
Professor Cheng Kai-ming’s long-standing call to “return learning to students” emphasises the need to cultivate self-regulated learners, active learners and a strong sense of student agency. From our frontline work through the Compassionate School Programme, we consistently see that such agency is not developed by technology alone. It grows in emotionally safe and respectful environments where students can identify feelings, express needs and build supportive peer relationships.
This reflects the principle often described as “Maslow before bloom”: when emotional needs are supported, higher-order learning becomes possible.
Three weeks ago, our team joined the Belt and Road Teacher Development Exchange Project in Shanghai, under the theme “Social and Emotional Learning for Teachers and Students in the AI Era”. After two days of largely one-way presentations in which participants remained seated with limited interaction, we led an experiential session that invited them to stand up, move around and engage in structured conversations designed to help them get to know one another, listen with curiosity and build authentic connection. Participants – academics, policymakers and officials from 22 countries – told us they wished such relational grounding had occurred on day one.
Experiencing this sense of psychological safety and human connection met their needs for trust, belonging and clarity – conditions that enabled them to participate more openly and make sense of AI’s implications in a more grounded way. Such relational readiness is often a prerequisite for deep learning, especially when navigating unfamiliar technologies.
For Hong Kong’s digital education blueprint to realise its promise, technological innovation must advance together with human connection. AI can support teachers by taking over routine tasks and expanding learning opportunities, but it cannot replace the relational conditions that make learning possible in the first place.
When teachers are equipped not only with digital competencies but also with social and emotional learning and compassionate communication skills, they are better able to notice students’ emotional cues, foster psychological safety and cultivate supportive peer relationships. This is how digital education becomes more than an upgrade in tools – it becomes a foundation for human growth.
In the age of AI, connection is not a “nice to have”; it is the condition that enables learning itself. That is what it means to build classrooms grounded in “connection before solution”.
Raymond Yang Sze-ngai, co-founder and executive director, Just Feel
International DSE an idea worth exploring – with caution
I refer to the letter, “Create an international DSE to extend Hong Kong’s educational reach” (November 3), which convincingly argues that Hong Kong should adapt its education system to a global context by promoting an internationalised Diploma of Secondary Education.
A primary strength of the proposal is the appeal of local universities to international students. The suggestion to model an International DSE on the International Baccalaureate is exactly what is needed to draw international students and benchmark our curriculum globally.
However, for an International DSE to be truly successful, local interests must be safeguarded. In the development of such a curriculum, the government must actively involve frontline educators, curriculum experts and the Hong Kong Examinations and Assessment Authority from the outset to ensure fairness and quality. What is essential is that the integrity of the local DSE is preserved, ensuring that the creation of an international curriculum in no way disadvantages local students.
If we can establish a globally recognised exam that benefits both international and local educational ecosystems, Hong Kong will solidify its status as a unique bridge between Eastern and Western teaching traditions.
Justin Ip, Sha Tin
Young Chinese need a more realistic view of marriage
It would appear China’s efforts to promote marriage by making it easier for people to register their marriage anywhere in the country are paying off, with the number of marriage registrations in the first three quarters of this year rising by 8.5 per cent year on year.
Besides the incentives and policy tweaks, education should also be a major tool to encourage young people to get married, have children and, most of all, enjoy married life.
Young people should learn to have realistic expectations of marriage. A marriage often starts on a high note, but that can’t last forever. After a while, things might become a little boring or even a bit sour. This is normal.
Public education should also highlight the joys of raising children. People should know that having a little girl or boy would give one at least as much pleasure as raising a dog or cat, and it lasts much longer too.
Roland Guettler, Lai Chi Kok

data.title: Japanese consumers fear curbs on eel delicacy ahead of global wildlife forum
data.description:
At an eel restaurant near Tokyo, four friends sit down to eat a Japanese delicacy now the subject of a heated international debate as its numbers decline.
The eel, hugely popular across Japan, is also endangered and will be discussed at a key global forum that regulates the trade of threatened wildlife, starting in Uzbekistan on Monday.
“It’s a luxury dish that we eat to treat ourselves or to celebrate an occasion,” Yukiko Takahashi, a 52-year-old saleswoman, said as she tucked into her food at Hiranuma Suisan.
“Today, we friends came here to lift our spirits.”
Japan is strongly opposed to a proposal by the European Union, Panama and Honduras to list all 17 species of eels worldwide under the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES), which restricts trade of protected wildlife.
Up to 85 per cent of global eel consumption occurs in East Asia, particularly in Japan, which imported almost three-quarters of the 61,000 tons consumed there last year.
Eel populations are falling worldwide, scientists say, largely due to factors linked to human activity, such as the pollution of waterways, destruction of wetlands, hydroelectric dams and fishing.

Estimating population sizes remains difficult because of the mysterious life cycle of the snakelike fish.
The Japanese eel, a common species in East Asia, reproduces west of the Mariana Islands, 2,000km to 3,000km (1,240 miles to 1,865 miles) from the Japanese coast.
Its larvae develop into glass eels, or juveniles, as they approach the coast, then live in freshwater for five to 15 years before making the reverse journey.
The Japanese eel, along with the American variety, is listed as endangered on the International Union for Conservation of Nature’s (IUCN) Red List.
The European eel is considered critically endangered.
Eels, called “unagi” in Japan, have been consumed there for millennia and are now typically grilled on skewers and dipped in a soy and rice wine sauce.
People are willing to face ever-increasing prices to continue the culinary tradition, driven by significant year-to-year fluctuations in supply.

“Customers understand that it’s a luxury dish,” chef Tomoyuki Takashino said from behind the stove as he skewered an eel he had just killed and filleted.
Unaju, an eel served over rice in a lacquered box, is priced at 5,250 yen (US$34), more than double what the dish might have cost 15 years ago, according to Takashino.
Nearly all eels consumed in Japan come from aquaculture, which relies entirely on the capture or import of glass eels because they do not reproduce in captivity.
Catches of glass eels in Japan have dropped to less than 10 per cent of their 1960s levels.
“We buy the glass eels, which we raise in these tanks,” said Takayuki Hiranuma, president of a hi-tech aquaculture farm that operates the restaurant.
Behind him, some 80,000 eels are kept in six huge reservoirs in 30 degrees Celsius filtered water.
Their weight will increase a thousandfold over 10 months of farming before they are sold or cooked on site.
If eels are listed under Appendix II of the CITES convention, as will be discussed in Uzbekistan, their trade would be more strictly regulated, requiring export permits from the country of origin.
“This is a point that worries us greatly,” Hiranuma said.

Japan argues that it has implemented fishing quotas and farming permits, and cites a study by Tokyo University of Marine Science and Technology that says the Japanese eel population has tripled across East Asia since 1990.
“And we are cooperating with China, South Korea and Taiwan – territories where the Japanese eel lives – to limit the quantities of glass eels captured and improve stock management,” said Tetsuya Kawashima, an official from the Japanese Fisheries Agency.
A United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization expert advisory panel also said in a report this year that the risk of extinction for Japanese and American eels was “low”.
But for Andrew Kerr of the Sustainable Eel Group, such findings failed to take into account eel trafficking, which he called the “greatest wildlife crime” on the planet.
Due to the fact that eels look alike, “all sorts of games are played pretending it’s one species when it’s another”, he said. “So trafficking can thrive.”
Asian supply has long relied on the smuggling of glass eels from the EU, which banned their export in 2010.
Around 100 tons of glass eels transit each year, according to the European police agency Europol.
But with the dismantling of illegal supply chains, European supply had diminished, and there had been a rise in shipments from the Caribbean, threatening that population, Kerr warned.
“It’s not about their imminent risk of extinction. It’s about how do we create some sort of world order in the trade of eel. It’s a global game,” he said.
